decisions.title: Agent spec 'model:' field semantics
decisions.context: Agent spec YAMLs may declare a model field with or without a provider prefix. packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1091-1097 implements parseModelSpec such that bare model ids (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6') default to provider='anthropic'. This structural pin means monitor classify calls attempt Anthropic-direct authentication regardless of the main session's configured provider. Under OpenRouter-only auth (the user's current configuration), every monitor's classify call fails with 'Expected either apiKey or authToken'. Issue-062 tracks this as the forcing artifact. All five bundled classifier agent YAMLs (hedge, commit-hygiene, fragility, work-quality, unauthorized-action) use bare model ids and are all affected. The decision is what 'model:' means in an agent spec at the framework level.
decisions.decision: Bare model ids in an agent spec resolve against the current session's configured provider via ExtensionContext. If ExtensionContext cannot supply a current-provider/current-model binding, the dispatch errors with a clear message naming the missing context. Fully-qualified model specs (provider/model) continue to pin the provider regardless of session. Pinning is explicit, not a default. Implementation prerequisite: research on ExtensionContext currentModel availability in pi-agent-core must complete first.
consequences: pi-behavior-monitors' classifyViaAgent must read the current session model from ExtensionContext at dispatch time, not from the agent YAML
parseModelSpec semantics change — bare specs route through ExtensionContext lookup instead of returning provider='anthropic' as a default
The five bundled classifier YAMLs can remain with bare model ids and will now follow whatever provider the user has configured
If ExtensionContext does not currently expose currentModel, that becomes a prerequisite unblocker — opens a scope question into pi-agent-core that must be verified before implementation
Workflow per-step provider override (issue-064) becomes the symmetric problem for workflow agents; same resolution applies

decisions.title: Thinking-seam enforcement for forced-toolChoice dispatch
decisions.context: Agent spec YAMLs can declare thinking: on, but packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1235-1248 does not forward thinking to the complete() call. The comment at line 1231 acknowledges that the Anthropic API rejects thinking + forced toolChoice, so thinking must stay off when the dispatch uses a phantom-tool verdict shape. All five bundled classifier YAMLs declare thinking: on despite the silent drop. Issue-063 tracks this dead config as the forcing artifact. The framework does not currently enforce consistency between spec-declared thinking and dispatch-time behavior.
decisions.decision: executeAgent in pi-jit-agents (once parseModelSpec and classify dispatch move there per DEC-0003) enforces thinking=off whenever toolChoice.type === 'tool' is present in the dispatch options. If the agent spec declares thinking: on in combination with forced toolChoice, the framework emits a warning naming the agent and the incompatibility, and proceeds with thinking disabled. Free-form (non-forced) dispatches continue to forward thinking per the spec. The forced-toolChoice path never silently drops thinking without notification.
consequences: executeAgent gains a pre-dispatch check that inspects toolChoice and the compiled spec's thinking field
The five bundled classifier YAMLs should be corrected to thinking: off for cleanliness, separately from the enforcement change
Any future agent that combines forced toolChoice with thinking: on will be flagged loudly rather than silently mis-executing
Warning channel must exist at the framework boundary — requires either a logger injected via DispatchContext or a structured diagnostic emitted alongside JitAgentResult

decisions.title: parseModelSpec ownership at the execute boundary
decisions.context: parseModelSpec lives in packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts today. It defaults bare model ids to provider='anthropic', pinning the monitor classify path to Anthropic regardless of session configuration. The same function logic (model-id parsing and provider selection) belongs at the execute boundary, which pi-jit-agents now owns per docs/planning/jit-agents-spec.md §2. Keeping it in pi-behavior-monitors creates duplication (issue-043, the forcing artifact) and embeds policy in the wrong layer. pi-workflows has the same structural question for its agent-step dispatch but with different currently-hidden behavior.
decisions.decision: Move parseModelSpec into pi-jit-agents as part of the executeAgent boundary. Remove the duplicate from pi-behavior-monitors. Update the consumer's classifyViaAgent to call the pi-jit-agents API. Audit pi-workflows for any implicit model-spec parsing in step dispatch; if found, consolidate there too. The function's new location must accommodate DEC-0001 (ExtensionContext lookup for bare specs) and DEC-0002 (thinking-seam enforcement).
consequences: pi-jit-agents gains a model-resolution surface at executeAgent — either as a helper function or inlined into the dispatch path
pi-behavior-monitors' classifyViaAgent shrinks — it calls executeAgent with a compiled spec and an ExtensionContext, no longer doing its own provider resolution
pi-workflows' agent-step dispatch audits for equivalent implicit parsing and consolidates there too
Requires DEC-0001 resolved before implementation, because the bare-spec semantics determine what parseModelSpec's new contract is

decisions.title: TraceEntry schema independence from SessionEntry
decisions.context: Issue-023 requires side-car trace capture for monitor classify calls. Pi-mono's SessionManager.SessionEntry (node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/session-manager.d.ts) carries the canonical tree structure (type discriminator, id, parentId, timestamp, extension fields). The architectural question: should TraceEntry inherit SessionEntry literally, or adopt its structural contract without tight coupling? Inherit-literally couples our trace evolution to pi-mono's session schema, so any upstream change auto-breaks traces. Adopt-structurally preserves pi-mono's tree-traversal patterns while keeping trace concerns scoped to the trace subsystem. The 2026-04-26 mandate-bound canonical-compliance audit established that the structural contract is canonical pi-mono and the inheritance is not — pi-coding-agent does not export SessionEntry as a base type for extension reuse, so structural adoption is the only path that does not invent a new pi-mono surface.
decisions.decision: Trace entries adopt the SessionEntry conceptual shape (type discriminator, id, parentId, timestamp, extension fields per kind) but live in a separate schema namespace. No literal inheritance, no shared base type. The trace subsystem owns its own discriminated union: session_start, classify_call, classify_response, context_collection, verdict_decision, trace_end. Both subsystems use identical timestamp semantics (ISO 8601 via new Date().toISOString()). If session schema evolves, traces continue independently.
consequences: TraceEntry is defined in pi-jit-agents and consumed by pi-behavior-monitors; not inherited from pi-coding-agent
Trace entries pass the same tree-traversal queries as session entries (getEntry / getBranch shape) if ever unified for analytics; that integration is opt-in and scoped to extension code
Both subsystems use identical timestamp semantics so cross-subsystem time correlation works without conversion
Redaction filter operates on both session and trace entries via a shared comprehensive pattern set
Schema evolution is independent: changes to SessionEntry do not force changes to TraceEntry and vice versa
A new .workflows/schemas/agent-trace.schema.json defines the trace shape; AJV validation reuses pi-project's schema-validator at write time

references.label: agent_end extension hook canonical capture point

decisions.title: Monitor observability via push-write trace stream (intentional divergence from pi-mono pull model)
decisions.context: Pi-mono SessionManager is pull-only: agents and extensions read session state via getBranch / getEntry / getEntries / getChildren but cannot write directly. SessionManager enforces readonly via the ReadonlySessionManager interface delivered to extensions (node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/session-manager.d.ts). Monitors are side-channel executors — classify calls fire on turn_end and message_end events outside the main agent loop — and require independent write capability to record execution traces. Issue-023 names the capture requirement. The architectural question is whether to align with pi-mono's pull/replay observability model (structurally impossible given ReadonlySessionManager) or to diverge to a push-observability surface scoped to monitors. The 2026-04-26 mandate-bound canonical-compliance audit verified that pi-mono provides zero push-observability primitives (no Tracer, no Telemetry, no Logger framework, only event subscription) and that the divergence is the only path that satisfies the capture requirement without violating ReadonlySessionManager.
decisions.decision: Monitors implement a separate push-observable trace subsystem (TraceWriter at the pi-jit-agents executeAgent boundary) that does NOT attempt to write to the session. This is an intentional divergence from pi-mono's pull-observable architecture. The divergence is justified by three forces: monitors are not agents; classify events should not pollute session history; ReadonlySessionManager structurally prevents the pull-aligned alternative. The trace subsystem is scoped to pi-behavior-monitors and pi-jit-agents and does not generalize to other extensions. Future upstream pi-mono push-observable surfaces (if added) do not force refactor — the separation is intentional and stable.
consequences: Pi-mono remains the system of record for agent conversation and tool execution (session pull-model)
Monitors are the system of record for classification events (trace push-model)
Consumers of monitor health and classification patterns query the trace directory, not the session
Trace writes pass through schema validation extending pi-project's schema-validator pattern — net-new write-time validation discipline because pi-mono SessionManager has no equivalent

Trace data may include credentials echoed by users in prompts; pi-mono has no redaction primitives so the trace subsystem ships a comprehensive redaction layer covering all known credential shapes plus a project-extension mechanism for domain-sensitive data

Bounded growth via date-rotation (.workflows/monitors/<name>/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl), 30-day rolling retention, 500MB-per-day overflow split — mirrors pi-coding-agent's timestamp-anchored session-file naming
Configuration via PI_AGENT_TRACE_DIR, PI_AGENT_TRACE_FILTER, PI_AGENT_TRACE_RETENTION_DAYS, PI_AGENT_TRACE_FILE_MAX_SIZE_MB env vars adopting pi-mono's canonical PI_* prefix convention
CLI flags --trace, --no-trace, --trace-dir <dir>, --trace-filter <file> mirroring pi-coding-agent's boolean / negation / value-arg patterns

references.label: agent_end extension hook canonical capture point

decisions.title: Sixth block of the substrate contract
decisions.context: The user's framing names the substrate contract as 'six discrete blocks.' Five enumerated against POC v2 evidence (config, partitions, lenses, closure-table relations, per-item macros). The sixth block of the contract is unresolved. Pre-heuristic candidates from the conversation were schemas-as-substrate, hierarchies-as-distinct-block, or macros split into registry + emitter. The heuristic-widening pass (mandates + monitors as in-scope typed contexts under one heuristic) narrows preferred candidates to two: prompt-composition contract OR scopes (user / project / agent). Implementation cannot proceed with contract enumeration until this resolves.
decisions.decision: Decision pending. Two heuristic-aligned candidates compete; three pre-heuristic candidates retained for completeness but not preferred. Resolution updates §1.1 + §2 of analysis/2026-05-03-substrate-arc-frame.md and gates downstream block-enumeration.

options_considered.label: Prompt-composition contract
options_considered.description: The rules governing how typed contexts compose into prompts: order, deduplication, override, applicability evaluation, token-budget allocation across mandates + lens views + raw blocks + monitor outputs. Made central by the heuristic-widening; was implicit in the integration map.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Heuristic-aligned; central API surface for the unified frame. Couples sixth-block to gap #34 (composition contract).

options_considered.label: Scopes (user / project / agent)
options_considered.description: Layered anchors with composition rules across scopes. Mandates live at user scope; project blocks at project scope; agent overrides at agent scope. Composition resolves layered. Foundational for scope-layering decision DEC-0010.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Heuristic-aligned; couples sixth-block to gap #37 (scope layering). Selection here cascades into discovery-anchor mechanics.

options_considered.label: Schemas-as-substrate (pre-heuristic)
options_considered.description: Every schema is itself a block, the meta-meta layer. Pre-heuristic candidate from POC v2 evaluation.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Not preferred under heuristic-widening; surfaced for completeness.

options_considered.label: Hierarchies-as-distinct-block (pre-heuristic)
options_considered.description: Hierarchy declarations elevate from a config field to their own typed block. Pre-heuristic candidate.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Not preferred under heuristic-widening; surfaced for completeness.

options_considered.label: Macros-split (pre-heuristic)
options_considered.description: Per-item macros + a separate cluster/whole-block render layer as separate blocks.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Not preferred under heuristic-widening; surfaced for completeness.

consequences: Superseded: off-by-one re-count of the substrate contract. Original framing claimed six discrete blocks while enumerating five (config + partitions + lenses + closure-table relations + per-item macros). On reflection no sixth block exists; the contract is five blocks. Filed under proliferation pressure during the heuristic-widening pass; retired without enactment.

decisions.title: Composition contract for prompt assembly across typed contexts
decisions.context: Under the unifying heuristic, multiple typed-context entries (lenses, mandates, monitor outputs, raw blocks) can land in one agent's prompt. The integration-map design assumed pre-rendered injection without articulating how N typed contexts compose. Order, deduplication, override semantics, applicability-evaluation timing, and token-budget allocation are unspecified. This is the central API surface for the unified heuristic; lens injection, mandate injection, monitor-output injection all flow through it. Surfaced as gap #34 in the heuristic-widening pass.
decisions.decision: Decision pending. The composition contract must specify: (1) ordering rules across context types; (2) deduplication when multiple entries pull the same item; (3) override semantics including agent-scope opt-out of mandates / lenses; (4) applicability-predicate evaluation timing (compile-time vs per-turn); (5) token-budget allocation policy with truncation strategy. Resolution lands as a new design doc plus DEC enactment; gates implementation of jit-agents lens-injection path and downstream typed-context surfaces.
consequences: Superseded: composition rules for prompt assembly (ordering, deduplication, override, applicability evaluation, token-budget allocation) are specification work, not a directional choice between mandate-compliant alternatives. Belongs as an FGAP or implementation-issue series, not a DEC. Filed by treating 'things that need specification' as 'decisions to make'; retired without enactment.

decisions.title: contextBlocks parameterization: typed object form vs bare-string convention
decisions.context: The integration-map design proposed a bare-string `_lens:<id>` convention for lens-view injection in agent contextBlocks. The per-item-macros plan (analysis/2026-05-02-per-item-macros-atomic-plans.md plan #3) extends contextBlocks to a typed object form `{name, item, focus, depth}`. Two parallel parameterization mechanisms in one field. The heuristic-widening pass forces typed-only because bare-string cannot carry the parameter set the unified surface needs: `_mandate:<id>`, `_lens:<id>?status=open`, `_monitor:<id>:last-classification`, agent-scope omit lists, applicability filters. Bare-string would proliferate as `_kind:id:opt1:opt2` — a regression toward stringly-typed configuration. Surfaced as gap #29.
decisions.decision: Typed object form of contextBlocks entries (name + optional item/focus/depth) is the canonical injection surface for selective composition. Bare-string form (whole-block injection by name) remains valid for backward compatibility with existing agent YAMLs. The heuristic-widening forces the typed form because bare-string cannot carry the parameter set the unified composition surface needs (lens id, item selector, focus, depth, applicability predicate). Per-item-macros plan #3 is the canonical landing path.

options_considered.label: Typed object form only (proposed direction)
options_considered.description: Single typed surface. Carries kind, id, scope, applicability, focus, depth, filters. Bare-string remains valid for whole-block injection only.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Slightly more verbose at the simple case (whole-block injection unchanged; lens injection requires typed entry). Single canonical surface; no fragmentation.

options_considered.label: Bare-string convention layered on typed (coexistence)
options_considered.description: Both surfaces accepted. Bare-string for trivial cases; typed for parameterized.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Two surfaces to maintain. Author must learn when to use which. Composition contract (DEC-0007) more complex.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Heuristic prefers one canonical surface; coexistence creates the same parallel-ungated-path pattern the substrate arc retires.

consequences: per-item-macros plan #3 (typed contextBlocks extension) is the canonical landing path; no parallel bare-string convention
Lens-view injection lands as `{kind: 'lens', id: 'context-management', filters: {status: 'open'}}` not `_lens:context-management`
Mandate injection lands as `{kind: 'mandate', id: 'mandate-001'}` or `{kind: 'mandate', filter: {tags: ['workflow']}}`
Monitor output injection lands as `{kind: 'monitor', id: 'fragility', focus: 'last-classification'}`
Filter parameterization channel (gap #30) collapses into the typed form's `filters` field

decisions.title: FGAP-001 resolution: closure-table relations vs subpath block names
decisions.context: FGAP-001 (hierarchical/nested block storage) was originally framed with proposed_resolution = 'extend block-api to support subpath block names (e.g. readBlock(features/consumer-migration)).' The substrate-arc POC v2 demonstrates closure-table relations as an alternative substrate for hierarchy: (parent, child, relation_type) edges over flat blocks, with multiple parallel hierarchies coexisting as different relation_type values. The heuristic-widening pass (mandates and monitors as in-scope typed contexts) tilts decisively toward closure-table-only because the heuristic prefers ONE storage primitive across all context types — mandates and monitors do not need subpath storage.
decisions.decision: Closure-table relations are the canonical primitive for hierarchical decomposition over project blocks. Subpath block names (FGAP-001's original framing) are not pursued. The heuristic prefers ONE storage primitive across all context types (project blocks, mandates, monitor specs). The phases-pattern subdirectory walks (readBlockDir + buildIdIndex's phases handling) remain as legacy exceptions, not generalized. POC at analysis/poc-degree-zero-lens/ proves the closure-table shape mechanically; FGAP-001's resolution path is now closure-table-only via a relations.json block kind landing in pi-project.

options_considered.label: Closure-table relations only (proposed)
options_considered.description: All hierarchy expressed as edges in relations.json over flat blocks. One storage primitive across all context types per heuristic.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Per-item lock contention unchanged from today (per-block file lock). Hierarchy queries require edge-walk per query (mitigated by SDK caching). Authoring experience: edge-write rather than file-tree write.

options_considered.label: Subpath block names only (original FGAP-001 framing)
options_considered.description: readBlock('features/consumer-migration/stories/STORY-A'). Native tree shape on disk. Atomic per-item writes.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Two storage forms across the contract (subpath for hierarchy, flat-with-edges for everything else). Block-api gains directory-walk semantics. Schema-walk targeting array-item subschema is a new contract.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Heuristic prefers one storage primitive; subpath form would create a parallel ungated path for hierarchy expression that the closure-table primitive already handles.

options_considered.label: Both (closure-table for cross-cutting taxonomies, subpath for work-decomposition)
options_considered.description: Hybrid: closure-table for lens-style organization, subpath for genuine work-decomposition with atomic-write requirements.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Dual maintenance burden. Two query paths, two write paths, two validation paths.
options_considered.rejected_reason: Heuristic prefers one canonical substrate; hybrid creates the same fragmentation the unifying frame is meant to retire.

consequences: FGAP-001's proposed_resolution field updates to closure-table-only direction on enactment
Subpath block names not implemented; readBlockDir's existing one-off subdirectory walks (phases pattern) remain as exceptions, not generalized
Work decomposition (FEAT-001 epics → stories → tasks, future PHASE-NNN structures) lives as flat blocks with closure-table edges
Lock contention claim from FGAP-001 framing unchanged: per-block file lock; partition-per-file unblock is not pursued under this direction
block-validation.ts rollback contract simpler: no recursion into subdirectories

decisions.title: Scope discovery and layering across user / project / agent anchors
decisions.context: GitHub issue #3 asked for 'configuration for folder placement' — relocating .project/ + .workflows/. The POC v2 closes the data-relocation surface via config.root. The heuristic-widening pass widens this to a layered-scope decision because mandates live at user scope (~/.claude/mandates.jsonl) while project blocks live at project scope (<config.root>). Agent-scope overrides (e.g., classifier X opts out of mandates Y, Z) form a third layer. Composition rules across scopes are unspecified: which scope wins on conflict, how config-discovery walks scopes, whether the config file itself is relocatable (or fixed at .project/). Surfaced as gap #37, expansion of gap #24.
decisions.decision: Decision pending. Composition rule must specify: (1) discovery anchor for the config file itself — `.project/config.json` always vs env-var (PI_PROJECT_ROOT) vs package.json field vs walk-up; (2) layered config merge semantics across user → project → agent (project overrides user; agent filters within composed result, with explicit allow-list / deny-list); (3) scope assignment per block kind (mandates user-scope; project blocks project-scope; monitor specs project-scope or split). Resolution lands as DEC enactment plus updates to §1.1 and §5 of the substrate-arc-frame doc.

options_considered.label: `.project/config.json` always anchored
options_considered.description: Config file fixed at `.project/`; only data files relocate via config.root. Issue #3 closes for data, not for config-file location.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Predictable; no env-var discovery. User who literally wants no `.project/` directory at all not served.

options_considered.label: Env-var discovery (e.g., PI_PROJECT_ROOT)
options_considered.description: Config relocatable via environment. Substrate fully unanchored from `.project/`.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Requires env state; users who don't set the var get default `.project/`. Forking and CI environments must surface the var.

options_considered.label: package.json field (e.g., `pi.project.root`)
options_considered.description: Declarative; no env state. Lives in repo's package.json.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Couples pi config surface to npm package surface. Non-npm projects need an alternative.

options_considered.label: Walk-up discovery (find .project/config.json or named alternative in nearest ancestor)
options_considered.description: Discovery follows git-root convention; first-found wins.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Implicit anchor; may surprise users in nested-project contexts.

consequences: Superseded: the real concern (GitHub #3 folder placement) is already closed empirically by the POC's config.root field. The user/project/agent scope-layering expansion was theoretical embellishment from the heuristic-widening pass — not a question anyone asked. Mandates being user-scope is a fact, not a decision. Filed by lumping a closed folder-placement question together with scope-layering theory; retired without enactment.

decisions.title: Retire packaged defaults — pi-project ships no seed schemas or seed block files
decisions.context: pi-project today ships default schemas + default block files under packages/pi-project/defaults/{schemas,blocks}/, copied into newly-initialized projects by initProject (packages/pi-project/src/index.ts:215-236). The seeding pattern is a recurring source of architectural debt: stale seeded files cannot be updated by package upgrades. The original seedExamples copy-on-first-run pattern in pi-behavior-monitors had this exact failure mode and was retired in favor of live three-tier discovery (packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:325-352, doc-comment explicitly cites the retirement). Even pi-project's additive-only initProject creates the same divergence the moment a default ships an updated version: the file exists in the user's project from a prior install and never updates. The embedded note in FGAP-001's proposed_resolution surfaced this as 'decision pending' but the directional choice is not pending; the decision has been made and needs first-class reification so forward work does not continue down the already-decided-against path.
decisions.decision: pi-project does not ship default schemas or default block files. Users install desired schemas and starter blocks via an opt-in mechanism — concrete shape of the install mechanism is unspecified but the directional choice is final: no auto-seeding, no copy-on-init defaults shipped from the package. Bundled defaults are retired. This applies to all future schema and block kinds (config, relations, etc.) and to the existing default set under packages/pi-project/defaults/ once the opt-in mechanism replaces it.
consequences: issue-074's framing changes from 'fix seedExamples short-circuit' to 'the seeded artifacts must not exist; remove the source of the recurring class of issues'.

The substrate-arc subagent's recommended bundle 1 (Substrate seeding) is misframed: shipping config.schema.json + relations.schema.json + default config.json + default relations.json into packages/pi-project/defaults/ violates this decision. The POC's outputs ship as documentation/examples users copy, not as auto-seeded defaults.
packages/pi-project/defaults/ existing contents are grandfathered until the opt-in install mechanism is specified; no new content lands there.
initProject's default-copying loops at packages/pi-project/src/index.ts:215-236 are scheduled for retirement once the opt-in mechanism replaces them.

Future schema/block updates flow to users via the opt-in mechanism, not via package upgrades silently changing on-disk content. This eliminates the entire class of stale-seeded-file divergence issues at the source.
FGAP-001's proposed_resolution embedded note 'we might ship no defaults at all, and/or move defaults to a separate install step users enact if they want. Decision pending.' is superseded by this DEC.

references.label: Original embedded note in FGAP-001 proposed_resolution preserving user wording
pi-behavior-monitors live three-tier discovery (the retirement parallel)

decisions.title: Phase/item dependency authoring shape: inline depends_on with edge projection vs edges-only authoring
decisions.context: The roadmap/plan substrate proposal requires expressing per-phase ordering (phase A before phase B in a roadmap) and per-item ordering (task A before task B in a plan). DEC-0009 mandates closure-table edges as the canonical primitive for hierarchical decomposition, which covers ordering. Two authoring shapes are mandate-compliant under DEC-0009 — both ship ordering as edges; difference is the user-facing surface for declaring them. The proposal flagged this as a forking choice that user must resolve before implementation step 4 (loadRoadmap) lands.

options_considered.label: Inline depends_on with SDK projection
options_considered.description: roadmap.schema.json's phases items + plan.schema.json's items each carry a depends_on: string[] field. At validation time, the SDK projects these to closure-table edges in relations.json with relation_type='phase_depends_on' or 'plan_item_depends_on'. Edges become the canonical storage; inline depends_on is authoring sugar. validateRoadmaps / validatePlans check inline-vs-edge consistency and emit drift diagnostics if they disagree.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: ergonomic authoring (depends_on lives where the phase is defined; one-place edits). Con: two surfaces for the same data; sync logic needed; drift surface; edge writes must check inline-syntactic-sugar fields too.

options_considered.label: Edges-only authoring
options_considered.description: roadmap.schema.json's phases items + plan.schema.json's items omit depends_on entirely. All ordering lives in relations.json with the new relation_types. Authoring a roadmap dependency means appending an edge via append-block-item with relation_type='phase_depends_on'; SDK reads + topo-sorts purely from edges.
options_considered.tradeoffs: Pro: single source of truth; uniform with how lens-curate works (edges are the canonical write surface). Con: less local — to see what phase A depends on, must query relations.json under the relation_type filter.

consequences: Whichever option enacts becomes the canonical authoring contract for every future composition that needs ordering (sequence of decisions, dependency graph between research entries, etc.).

Inline option requires SDK validators to enforce sync between inline fields and edges; edges-only option requires authoring tools (or a /project add-dep convenience subcommand) to make ordering authoring ergonomic from a session.
Inline option is friendlier to fresh users who haven't internalized the closure-table model; edges-only option is more consistent with the substrate's stated principle (one storage primitive).
